title: Electricity and Energy
subtitle: Circuits, current and voltage, series and parallel, energy stores, transfers and resources.

studyGuide.heading: Circuits and symbols
studyGuide.html:
A circuit needs a complete loop and a power source. Key components:

  • Cell / battery — pushes the current (provides energy).
  • Bulb / lamp — transfers energy to light.
  • Switch — opens or closes the circuit.
  • Ammeter — measures current (in amps, A), connected in series.
  • Voltmeter — measures voltage (in volts, V), connected in parallel across a component.

diagram.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 240 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.w{stroke:#1f2a44;stroke-width:2;fill:none}.t{font:10px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}</style><rect x='20' y='30' width='200' height='80' class='w'/><line class='w' x1='105' y1='30' x2='105' y2='15'/><line class='w' x1='135' y1='30' x2='135' y2='15'/><line class='w' x1='105' y1='15' x2='135' y2='15'/><line class='w' x1='112' y1='20' x2='112' y2='10'/><line class='w' x1='128' y1='25' x2='128' y2='15'/><text class='t' x='150' y='18'>cell</text><circle cx='120' cy='110' r='12' class='w'/><line class='w' x1='112' y1='102' x2='128' y2='118'/><line class='w' x1='128' y1='102' x2='112' y2='118'/><text class='t' x='95' y='135'>bulb</text></svg>
diagram.caption: A simple series circuit: cell and bulb in one loop.

studyGuide.heading: Current and voltage
studyGuide.html:
• Current is the flow of electric charge, measured in amps (A) with an ammeter (in series).
  • Voltage (potential difference) is the 'push' from the cell, measured in volts (V) with a voltmeter (in parallel).
  • More cells (higher voltage) → bigger current → brighter bulbs.
  • Adding more bulbs in series shares the voltage, so each bulb is dimmer.
studyGuide.mnemonic: Ammeter in series (the current goes Along through it); Voltmeter in parallel (sits aVer the component).

studyGuide.heading: Series circuits
studyGuide.html:
• Components are in one single loop.
  • The current is the same everywhere in the loop.
  • The voltage is shared between the components.
  • If one bulb breaks, the circuit is broken and all bulbs go out.

studyGuide.heading: Parallel circuits
studyGuide.html:
• Components are on separate branches.
  • The voltage is the same across each branch.
  • The current splits between the branches.
  • If one bulb breaks, the others stay on — this is why house wiring is parallel.

studyGuide.heading: Conductors and insulators
studyGuide.html:
• Conductors let current flow easily — most metals (copper, steel) and graphite.
  • Insulators do not let current flow — plastic, rubber, glass, wood.
  • Wires are copper (conductor) coated in plastic (insulator) for safety.

studyGuide.heading: Energy stores and transfers
studyGuide.html:
Energy is never made or destroyed — it is transferred between stores (law of conservation of energy).

  • Stores: kinetic (movement), chemical (food, fuel, batteries), gravitational (height), elastic (stretched), thermal (hot objects).
  • Transferred by: electricity, light, sound, heating, and forces (mechanically).
  • Useful energy + 'wasted' energy (often heat/sound) = total energy in.

studyGuide.heading: Energy resources
studyGuide.html:
• Renewable (won't run out): solar, wind, hydroelectric, tidal, geothermal, biomass.
  • Non-renewable (will run out): fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas) and nuclear.
  • Fossil fuels release carbon dioxide, a greenhouse gas linked to climate change.

flashcards.front: What does an ammeter measure and how is it connected?
flashcards.back: Current in amps (A); connected in series.

flashcards.front: What does a voltmeter measure and how is it connected?
flashcards.back: Voltage in volts (V); connected in parallel across a component.
The same everywhere in the loop.
The whole circuit breaks — all bulbs go out.
Energy is never created or destroyed, only transferred or stored.
Solar, wind, hydroelectric, tidal (also geothermal, biomass).
Fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas) and nuclear.

flashcards.front: Main store in a stretched spring

commonMistakes.title: Connecting an ammeter in parallel
commonMistakes.detail: Ammeter goes in SERIES (current flows through it); voltmeter goes in parallel.

commonMistakes.title: Saying current 'gets used up' in series
commonMistakes.detail: Current is the same all the way round a series circuit; it is the energy/voltage that is shared.

commonMistakes.title: Saying energy is 'made' or 'used up'
commonMistakes.detail: Energy is transferred or stored, never created or destroyed. 'Wasted' energy is still there, usually as heat.

commonMistakes.title: Calling nuclear renewable
commonMistakes.detail: Nuclear is non-renewable (uranium runs out), though it is low-carbon.

commonMistakes.title: Mixing up volts and amps
commonMistakes.detail: Voltage (V) is the push; current (A) is the flow.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting why house circuits are parallel
commonMistakes.detail: So each appliance gets full voltage and can be switched on/off independently.

questions.q: What does an ammeter measure?
questions.explanation: An ammeter measures current in amps.
questions.hint: Amps — the clue is in the name.

questions.q: A voltmeter is connected…
questions.explanation: A voltmeter is connected in parallel across the component.
questions.hint: It sits across the component.

questions.q: Which material is an insulator?
questions.explanation: Plastic does not conduct electricity — it is an insulator.
questions.hint: What coats electrical wires for safety?

questions.q: In a series circuit, if one bulb breaks, the other bulbs…
questions.explanation: Breaking the single loop stops the current, so all bulbs go out.
questions.hint: There's only one loop.

questions.q: The unit of voltage is the…
questions.hint: Volt-age.

questions.q: Which is a renewable energy resource?
questions.explanation: Wind is renewable — it won't run out.
questions.hint: Which one keeps being replaced naturally?

questions.q: Name the energy store in a battery.
questions.hint: Same store as in food and fuels.
questions.examMistake: Saying 'electrical' — that's the transfer, not the store inside the battery.

questions.q: State what 'current' means.
questions.modelAnswer: Current is the flow of electric charge around a circuit, measured in amps.
questions.hint: Think of it as a flow.

questions.q: What happens to the current as you add more cells to a circuit?
questions.explanation: More cells = more voltage = bigger current, so bulbs are brighter.
questions.hint: More push means more flow.

questions.q: In a parallel circuit, if one branch bulb breaks, the others…
questions.explanation: Other branches still have a complete loop, so they stay on.
questions.hint: Each branch is separate.

questions.q: Why are electrical wires made of copper coated in plastic?
questions.modelAnswer: Copper is a good conductor so it lets the current flow easily, and the plastic is an insulator that stops you getting a shock and stops wires touching.
questions.hint: One part carries current, the other keeps you safe.
questions.examMistake: Only naming the materials without saying conductor/insulator.

questions.q: Energy can be…
questions.explanation: Energy is conserved — it is transferred, not created or destroyed.
questions.hint: Conservation of energy.

questions.q: Explain why two bulbs in series are dimmer than one bulb on its own with the same cell.
questions.modelAnswer: In a series circuit the voltage from the cell is shared between the components. With two bulbs, each bulb only gets half the voltage, so less energy is transferred to each one and they glow more dimly than a single bulb that gets the full voltage.
questions.hint: What is shared between the bulbs?
questions.examMistake: Saying the current is 'used up' by the first bulb — it isn't.

questions.q: In a series circuit, the current at point A (before a bulb) is 0.3 A. What is the current after the bulb?
questions.explanation: Current is the same everywhere in a series circuit, so it is still 0.3 A.
questions.hint: Series = same current everywhere.

questions.q: Describe two reasons household circuits are wired in parallel rather than series.
questions.modelAnswer: First, each appliance gets the full mains voltage so it works properly. Second, each can be switched on and off independently, and if one appliance or bulb fails the others keep working because each is on its own branch.
questions.hint: Think about voltage and what happens when one fails.
questions.examMistake: Giving only one reason when two are asked for.

questions.q: Which energy transfer happens in a torch (flashlight)?
questions.explanation: Chemical store in the battery transfers electrically to the bulb, then to light (and wasted heat).
questions.hint: Start with the battery's store.

questions.q: A roller coaster car at the top of a hill has a gravitational store. Describe the energy transfers as it goes down and speeds up.
questions.modelAnswer: As the car goes down, energy is transferred from the gravitational store to the kinetic store, so it speeds up. Some energy is also transferred to thermal (heat) and sound stores by friction and air resistance, which is 'wasted'. The total energy stays the same.
questions.hint: Where does the height energy go as it speeds up?
questions.examMistake: Forgetting that some energy is wasted as heat/sound.

questions.q: Why is nuclear power classed as non-renewable even though it is low-carbon?
options: The uranium fuel will eventually run out
questions.explanation: Uranium is a finite fuel that will run out, so nuclear is non-renewable, though it emits little CO2.
questions.hint: Will the fuel last forever?

questions.q: Explain the difference between current and voltage.
questions.modelAnswer: Current is the flow of electric charge around the circuit, measured in amps with an ammeter in series. Voltage (potential difference) is the energy or 'push' supplied by the cell to drive that current, measured in volts with a voltmeter in parallel. Voltage causes the current to flow.
questions.hint: One is the flow, one is the push.
questions.examMistake: Treating volts and amps as the same thing.

questions.q: In a parallel circuit the main current is 0.6 A and splits equally between two identical branches. Current in each branch?
questions.explanation: Current splits between branches: 0.6 A ÷ 2 = 0.3 A each.
questions.hint: It splits between the branches.

questions.q: Give one advantage and one disadvantage of using solar panels to generate electricity.
questions.modelAnswer: Advantage: solar is renewable and produces no carbon dioxide while generating, so it does not add to climate change. Disadvantage: it only works in daylight/sunny conditions, so it is unreliable, and the panels are expensive to install.
questions.hint: Think clean but weather-dependent.
questions.examMistake: Giving two advantages or two disadvantages instead of one each.

questions.q: A circuit has a cell, an open switch and a bulb in series. The bulb is off. Why?
options: The open switch breaks the loop so no current flows
questions.explanation: An open switch leaves a gap, breaking the circuit so no current flows.
questions.hint: What does an open switch do to the loop?

questions.q: A 3 V cell lights two identical bulbs. Compare the brightness and the voltage across each bulb when they are connected (a) in series and (b) in parallel.
questions.modelAnswer: In series, the 3 V is shared, so each bulb has about 1.5 V across it and both are dim. In parallel, each branch gets the full 3 V across each bulb, so each bulb is bright (as bright as a single bulb). Parallel bulbs are brighter because they each receive the full voltage.
questions.hint: How is the 3 V divided in each arrangement?
questions.examMistake: Saying parallel bulbs share the voltage — they each get the full voltage.

questions.q: Use the idea of energy conservation to explain why an old-style filament bulb is described as 'inefficient'.
questions.modelAnswer: All the electrical energy supplied is transferred, and energy is conserved. In a filament bulb only a small fraction is transferred to the useful light store; most is transferred to the surroundings as wasted heat (thermal store). Because so little of the input energy becomes useful light, the bulb is inefficient.
questions.hint: Where does most of the energy actually go?
questions.examMistake: Saying energy is 'lost' — it is transferred to heat, not destroyed.

questions.q: Two ammeters in a series circuit read different values (0.4 A and 0.2 A). What is the most likely explanation?
options: There is a measurement/fault error — series current must be equal
questions.explanation: Current is identical everywhere in series, so unequal readings indicate a faulty meter or wrong connection.
questions.hint: What should series current readings be?

questions.q: Explain why burning fossil fuels for electricity contributes to climate change, and suggest one way to reduce this.
questions.modelAnswer: Burning fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas) releases carbon dioxide, a greenhouse gas that traps heat in the atmosphere and causes global warming and climate change. This can be reduced by switching to renewable resources such as wind or solar, which generate electricity without releasing carbon dioxide.
questions.hint: Which gas, what does it do, and a cleaner alternative.
questions.examMistake: Naming a gas other than carbon dioxide as the main cause.

questions.q: A student claims 'the current is used up as it goes round the circuit, which is why the bulb lights'. Explain what is wrong and give the correct idea.
questions.modelAnswer: Current is not used up — the same current flows all the way round a series circuit and returns to the cell. What is actually transferred is energy: the cell gives the charges energy (voltage), and the bulb transfers that energy to light and heat as the charges pass through. The charges keep flowing; it is the energy that is transferred.
questions.hint: Distinguish 'current' from 'energy'.
questions.examMistake: The classic misconception that current is consumed by components.

questions.q: Why does adding a second cell in series make a bulb brighter, in terms of energy?
options: It adds more current that gets used up
It increases the total voltage, transferring more energy to the bulb each second
questions.explanation: Two cells provide more voltage, so more energy is transferred to the bulb per second, making it brighter.
questions.hint: More cells = more voltage = more energy transferred.

questions.q: Describe an investigation to find out whether different metals conduct electricity equally well, identifying the variables.
questions.modelAnswer: Set up a series circuit with a cell, an ammeter and a gap where you connect a test wire. Independent variable: type of metal (e.g. copper, iron, aluminium). Dependent variable: current shown on the ammeter. Control variables: same length and thickness of wire, same voltage/cells, same temperature. Connect each metal in turn, record the current, repeat and take a mean. A higher current means the metal is a better conductor.
questions.hint: Same wire dimensions, change only the metal.
questions.examMistake: Forgetting to keep wire length and thickness the same.

questions.q: A wind farm and a gas power station can both supply a town. Compare them in terms of reliability and environmental impact.
questions.modelAnswer: The gas station is reliable and can be turned up on demand, but it is non-renewable and releases carbon dioxide, adding to climate change. The wind farm is renewable and releases no carbon dioxide when generating, but it is unreliable because it only works when the wind blows, so it cannot guarantee supply. A mix is often used so wind reduces emissions while gas covers calm periods.
questions.hint: Trade-off: reliable but polluting vs clean but variable.
questions.examMistake: Only discussing one of reliability or environment, not both.
